title: Identifying and treating sarcoptic mange in pigs
subtitle: Recognising pig mange and the treatment approach for a small herd.
excerpt: Sarcoptic mange is common in pigs kept in close contact and spreads rapidly. It causes intense itching, skin thickening, and reduced growth rates. This covers the signs, confirmation, treatment options, and the precautions to take when treating pigs that may go to slaughter.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Pigs (2003, updated 2022). Pig Veterinary Society, mange management notes. AHDB Pork, pig health guide.

content.text: rub persistently against pen fixtures, fencing, and each other. The skin thickens and develops a grey, crusty appearance, beginning at the ears and spreading to the neck and flanks. In a pig with good condition, early mange may show only as mild skin thickening and occasional rubbing; confirm before treating.

A vet can take a skin scraping and check it under a microscope to confirm mites. In most cases you can start treatment from the field signs alone: steady rubbing, ear discharge, and thick skin in several pigs sharing a pen. Lab confirmation is useful when you can get it. For a small herd it is not always practical. Contact your vet for prescription treatments.
Treat the entire pen group, not just visibly affected individuals.

by injection or in-feed formulation is the standard treatment; both require a vet prescription and both have withdrawal periods that must be observed before slaughter. Record the treatment date, product, and withdrawal period in the medicine book. Two treatments two weeks apart are typically required to catch adult mites and newly hatched larvae from eggs unaffected by the first dose.

Mites survive off-host in bedding for two to three weeks. Clean and disinfect the pen between treatment rounds using a product with documented efficacy against Sarcoptes mites; your vet will recommend a suitable product. New pigs introduced to the herd should be treated and isolated for at least three weeks before mixing.
